title: omega has no theory of Nat.gcd, fails on gcd a b | a
informal_statement: The gcd of a and b divides a, for all naturals a, b: Nat.gcd a b | a, attacked by omega directly instead of the dedicated library lemma Nat.gcd_dvd_left.
formal_statement_pp: theorem root_theorem : ∀ a b : ℕ, Nat.gcd a b ∣ a

failure.input_context_summary: Goal `Nat.gcd a b ∣ a` over ℕ after `intro a b`.
failure.attempt_summary: Applied `omega` directly. `omega` treats `a.gcd b` as an opaque atom with no built-in theory of `Nat.gcd` at all -- not even a weak approximation -- and reports a spurious counterexample (`c := a % (a.gcd b) >= 1`, i.e. it cannot even establish that the atom evenly divides `a`). kernel_fail (tactic_failure, ~15.5s).
failure.lesson: omega's decision procedure covers linear arithmetic over ℤ/ℕ (plus a limited amount of `%`/`/` reasoning on literal-valued divisors); it has no semantic knowledge of `Nat.gcd` whatsoever, so any goal mentioning `gcd` needs to route through the gcd API directly (`Nat.gcd_dvd_left`, `Nat.gcd_dvd_right`, `Nat.dvd_gcd`, etc.) rather than expecting omega to unfold or approximate it. Unlike the domain's other omega-failure examples (an unevaluated-literal divisor, a nonlinear `n^2` atom), this is a total theory gap, not a normalization or nonlinearity issue -- omega cannot make ANY progress on a bare gcd atom, evidenced by the trivial single-hypothesis counterexample class it reports.

notes: Produced via tracked proofsearch episode e92f1fe2-eedb-48d5-94c8-9695aa253fed (problem_version 70addb1d-32b8-4fe4-b2f4-69e1ff0b5019): `solve` with `intro a b; omega` returned kernel_fail (tactic_failure, omega's spurious-counterexample report on the bare gcd atom); the same episode's second step, `intro a b; exact Nat.gcd_dvd_left a b`, reached kernel_verified -- but that exact theorem already existed in the corpus as `packets/elementary/number_theory/gcd_dvd_left.v1.json`, so no duplicate positive packet was authored; this negative example references that existing packet as its companion instead. Third negative example for the number_theory domain, alongside divisor_case_split_omega_unevaluated_literal.v1 and sq_parity_omega_nonlinear_failure.v1 -- all three are distinct omega-failure sub-categories (unevaluated literal, nonlinear atom, total theory gap) rather than near-duplicates.
